coredata.dc:title: Investigation on the impact of green synthesized N-C-dots decoration over photocatalytic efficiency of S-g-C3N4/ZnO

coredata.dc:description: Herein, green synthesized N-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) and in-situ produced binary composites of sulphur-doped graphitic carbon nitride (S-g-C3N4) and ZnO have been hydrothermally treated to develop their ternary composite, i.e., N-C-dots/S-g-C3N4/ZnO (N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO) to investigate the impact of N-C-dots decoration over SgCN/ZnO for photocatalytic decomposition of rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The obtained N-C-dots are almost spherical and exhibited remarkable crystalline properties, with particles diameters ranging from 6.21 to 12.56 nm. The obtained ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO showed noticeably higher photocatalytic effectiveness than its constituents' components. Additionally, N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated outstanding photocatalytic decomposition (93.49 %) of RhB dye under optimized conditions in only 35 min. Various dye degradation parameters like photocatalyst dose, dye concentration, pH of dye solution have been optimized for RhB degradation along with impacts of different scavengers. The results of capturing experiments revealed that h+, •O2 − and •OH radicals are main components for photocatalytic breakdown of dye with O2 • − rendering the major degradation, whereas •OH and h+ have minor involvement. N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated outstanding reusability up to five cycles by degrading 85.54 % RhB dye in 5th cycle in just 35 min. Additionally, only slight variations are observed in XRD pattern of freshly produced and recycled sample, indicating exceptional ternary composite stability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, green synthesized N-doped carbon dots (N-C-dots) and in-situ produced binary composites of sulphur-doped graphitic carbon nitride (S-g-C3N4) and ZnO have been hydrothermally treated to develop their ternary composite, i.e., N-C-dots/S-g-C3N4/ZnO (N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO) to investigate the impact of N-C-dots decoration over SgCN/ZnO for photocatalytic decomposition of rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The obtained N-C-dots are almost spherical and exhibited remarkable crystalline properties, with particles diameters ranging from 6.21 to 12.56 nm. The obtained ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO showed noticeably higher photocatalytic effectiveness than its constituents' components. Additionally, N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated outstanding photocatalytic decomposition (93.49 %) of RhB dye under optimized conditions in only 35 min. Various dye degradation parameters like photocatalyst dose, dye concentration, pH of dye solution have been optimized for RhB degradation along with impacts of different scavengers. The results of capturing experiments revealed that h+, •O2 − and •OH radicals are main components for photocatalytic breakdown of dye with O2 • − rendering the major degradation, whereas •OH and h+ have minor involvement. N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated outstanding reusability up to five cycles by degrading 85.54 % RhB dye in 5th cycle in just 35 min. Additionally, only slight variations are observed in XRD pattern of freshly produced and recycled sample, indicating exceptional ternary composite stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The issue of environmental contamination is becoming increasingly problematic for human beings and aquatic system due to the extensive expansion of industries and modern civilization. Aquatic contaminants seriously threaten human beings and environmental [1]. Specifically, organic dyes namely malachite green, methylene blue, congo red, crystal violet, rhodamine B, etc., which are readily identified by their distinctive colour are the main contaminants in aquatic ecosystems and have the potential to produce hazardous byproducts during the wastewater phase through oxidation, hydrolysis, or other chemical processes [2]. Consequently, the effective degradation or elimination of these hazardous contaminants has emerged as a hotspot for wastewater treatment and ecological remediation research [3]. Among several traditional methods such as biodegradation, flocculation, adsorption, etc., photocatalytic dye decontamination technique is an appealing method for combining ecological restoration with energy transformation, which involves transformation of light energy into chemical energy and degrades contaminants into less harmful products while avoiding production of secondary pollutants [4].

From recent decade, g-C3N4 (gCN) has drawn a lot of interest from scientific community from all across the globe due to its appropriate band position and effective redox capability for production of hydrogen via water-splitting procedure and breakdown of organic waste contaminants and byproducts through photocatalysis [5]. It is an excellent metal-free photocatalyst owning to its layered structure, greater surface area to volume ratio, adequate optical band gap energy (2.7 eV), as well as its electron-rich surface [6]. Its exceptional physicochemical qualities such as light weight, chemical stability, biocompatible and water resistance nature make it ideal for the photocatalysis process. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic reports on pristine gCN are limited because of its poor efficacy in visible spectrum because of the rapid rate of reunion of electron- hole couples when exposed to light [7].

Several methods have been investigated to improve gCN's photocatalytic efficiency. By the conjugation method, scientists have combined gCN with several inorganic semiconductors e.g., ZnO, TiO2, CdS, etc., to broaden the separation between electron- hole couples and hence reducing their fast recombination and, as a result, to improve its photocatalytic efficiency [8]. ZnO is a type II semiconductor that is active in UV spectrum with optical band gap energy of 3.37 eV [9]. Due to greater optical band gap energy its practical uses in visible spectrum photocatalysis are limited. However, a number of investigations have shown that a ZnO and gCN composites demonstrate outstanding photocatalytic activity. For instances, Qamar et al., designed Ni/ZnO/gCN which demonstrated excellent photocatalytic decomposition (100 %) of MB dye when exposed to solar light in 70 min [10]. Suhag et al., reported the outstanding photocatalytic detoxification of bisphenol E by ZnO/gCN in visible light [11].

Furthermore, the ternary composite of ZnO and gCN with zero-dimensional biomass mediated C-dots have the possibility to further boost their photocatalytic capability for wastewater remediation by preventing the reunion of electrons-holes couples. A novel class of tiny carbon nanomaterials (NMs), C-dots have good photocatalytic properties because of their minimal toxicities, facile production and functionalization on the surface, and adaptable composition. Interestingly, they have electron-transport and accepting capabilities in addition to down- and up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) [12]. The first one makes it possible to use visible and near-UV spectrum of solar radiations, while the second one prevents electron-hole couple from recombining, increasing photocatalytic effectiveness [8]. Consequently, C-dots are being employed as desirable modifiers to increase the photocatalytic efficacy of various semiconductors, such as gCN, ZnO, and TiO2 [13,14]. There have been only few reports on production of ternary composite C-dots with in-situ fabricated SgCN/ZnO to examine the influence C-dots conjunction on photocatalytic performance of SgCN/ZnO for dye degradation.

Hence, we have explored the production of ternary composite of biomass-mediated N-doped C-dots from sweet potato with in-situ fabricated SgCN/ZnO to examine the influence of N-C-dots decoration on charge separation and hence photocatalytic efficiency of SgCN/ZnO against rhodamine B (RhB) dye decontamination. The green synthesis method adopted in the present study for the fabrication of N-C-dots is safe, easy and superior than other methods [15,16]. Because, no step involved in the synthesis process has any harmful environmental impact. In addition, the precursor used for the synthesis is biomass and hence by-products formed in the synthesis process is biochar which is biodegradable. The ternary composite i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO (N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO) showed superior photocatalytic decomposition of RhB dye in comparison to its individual components under identical conditions. It is because of effective charge separation brought about by decoration of N-C-dots over in-situ synthesized binary composite i.e., SgCN/ZnO. In addition, the influence of photocatalyst dose, dye concentration and pH of dye solution on photocatalytic performance of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO against RhB decomposition have also been investigated. The impact of different scavengers on photocatalytic degradation of RhB has also been investigated which revealed that h+, •O2 − and •OH radicals are main components for photocatalytic breakdown of dye with O2 • − rendering the major degradation, whereas •OH and h+ have minor involvement. Furthermore, to determine the long-term utility of the produced hybrid nanocomposite N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO, further investigations have been conducted on their reusability as well as photostability. Finally, a plausible photocatalytic dye decontamination pathway by N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO photocatalyst has been postulated.

The chemicals used, such as Zn(NO3)2.6H2O (99 % purity) from Sisco Research laboratories Pvt. Ltd. (SRL), liq. NH3 (30 % aqueous solution) from SRL, Na2EDTA (99.5 % purity) from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (CDH), thiourea (99.0 % purity) from CDH, tert-butanol (99.5 % purity) from CDH, p-benzoquinone (98.0 % purity) from Loba Chemie, and RhB dye from CDH, are all of analytical grade and purchased from India. All of the experimentation was conducted using double-deionized (DI) water. Each chemical had been utilized without any additional purification.

Sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) was utilized as biomass precursor for green production of C-dots. It was initially washed multiple times with double DI water and subsequently cut into tiny pieces and then dried for approximately 48 h in direct sunlight. It was then pyrolyzed for 4 h at 400 °C in a muffle furnace. The pyrolysis-derived material was subsequently ground into an incredibly fine powder using grinder. The 50 g of obtained powder was dispersed in 800 mL of double distilled water. The resulting mixture was agitated at 1200 rpm for 24 h. Then 12 mL of liquid NH3 (dopant) was poured into the mixture and it was agitated once more for 24 h. The resulting mixture was subsequently left unperturbed to allow the sedimentation of bigger particles for the following 24 h. After that, the filtrate was collected, and bigger particles have been eliminated even further through its centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 60 min. After centrifugation, the filtrate was collected and then passed through Whatman filter paper, which has a pore size of 2.5 μm. After filtration, the supernatant obtained was heated on a hot plate to completely evaporate the water. Ultimately, N-doped C-dots were obtained and finely powdered [17].

In-situ fabrication of SgCN/ZnO was carried out utilizing a traditional thermal pyrolysis procedure. 40 g of thiourea and 10 g of Zn(NO3)2.6H2O was manually mixed properly for 20 min and the mixture was subsequently put in 4 ceramic crucibles that were each tightly covered with multiple sheets of aluminum foil. Following that, crucibles have been put in muffle furnace for thermal pyrolysis of mixture at 540 °C for 4 h at temperature raising rate of 4 °C/min. Consequently, molecules of thiourea self-polymerized with the release of ammonia gas. The atoms of sulphur were added in-situ to the gCN network during the process of thermal polycondensation. Additionally, Zn(NO3)2.6H2O undergo dehydration and thermal decomposition to produce ZnO. Following the natural cooling of furnace, the resulting SgCN/ZnO sample, which had a brownish-yellow colour, was taken and finely powdered [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO hybrid nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of C-dot composites with metal oxide and other nanostructures are produced using the hydrothermal method. This approach has frequently been regarded as one of the easiest and most efficient techniques. Special tools and complex synthesis conditions are not necessary for this method. By regulating reaction variables including temperature, reactant concentration, and duration of reaction, it regulates the chemical composition, particles size, and morphological properties of the composites. Under typical circumstances, the hydrothermal method for synthesizing composites causes the reactant components to dissolve, nucleate, and crystallize. Reactions are often carried out for 4–8 h or longer at temperatures between 150 and 180 °C. However, it might change depending on the weight ratio, material utilized, and other factors. As per the literature, heating at 140 °C for 4 h is sufficient for green synthesized C-dots to get decorated over in-situ fabricated SgCN/ZnO [19,20]. A Teflon tube and an outer jacket are used in a standard hydrothermal method. Additionally, every hydrothermal outer jacket has a bottom disc, pressure plates, and an upper screw cap. Pressure plates are designed to withstand the high temperatures and pressure for an extended period of time [21]. Herein, 0.4 g of N-C-dots and 1.6 g of SgCN/ZnO (weight ratio 1:4) were mixed together in aqueous medium (50 mL) to form their homogeneous solution and agitated for 12 h. Then it was transferred to a 110 mL stainless steel autoclave and heated to 140 °C for 4 h in hot air oven for hydrothermal processing. It led to the production of ternary composite of N-C-dots and SgCN/ZnO i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO. Subsequently, the resultant mixture was dried for 12 h at 70 °C in hot air oven. Afterward, the ternary composite was taken out and finely powdered [22].

The structural properties of the produced NMs have been analyzed using a “Rigaku Ultima IV X-Ray Diffractometer” (XRD). Chemical composition and functional groups in the 400–4000 cm−1 region were investigated using X-rays Photon Spectrophotometer and FTIR, respectively. “Thermo Scientific's high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM)” has been employed to determine the surface morphological properties of the particles. Utilizing the “STR 500 Confocal Micro Raman Spectrometer,” the % purity of the obtained C-dots was evaluated. Utilizing a “BIOGENIX UV-vis Spectrophotometer” (Model: BS-UVD-2900), the optical and photocatalytic efficacy evaluation of the synthesized NMs was conducted. A “HORIBA Fluoromax Spectrofluorometer” has been utilized for photoluminescence (PL) investigations.

The photocatalytic capability of synthesized NMs were examined through photocatalytic decomposition of aqueous solution of RhB dye in the presence of 300 W UV lamp. RhB dye solutions were prepared in double-DI water, each with a predetermined concentration. A known amount of fabricated NMs was then introduced to 50 mL of RhB dye solutions. To achieve equilibrium conditions, the resultant solutions were first agitated for 30 min in dark environment. Subsequently, dye solutions were exposed to UV-radiations for a fixed duration. Periodically, 3 mL dye solutions were withdrawn to acquire UV–vis absorption spectra for further examination. The highest intensity of absorption for RhB dye has been obtained at 554 nm in UV–vis absorption spectra [17].

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of N-C-dots and SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO. The XRD pattern of sweet potato-derived N-C-dots indicates its crystalline structure having sharp peaks at 2θ values of 28.39o, 40.64o, 50.12o, 58.67o, 66.35o, and 73.72o which corresponds to (002), (100), (102), (600), (220) and (215) planes [23,24]. In-situ synthesized SgCN/ZnO exhibited characteristics XRD peaks corresponding to both SgCN as well as ZnO as shown in Fig. 1. The XRD peaks at 2θ values of 12.88o and 27.60o are correlated to (110) and (002) plane of SgCN, respectively [8], while other peaks centered at 2θ values 31.85o, 34.41o, 36.21o, 47.54o, 56.73o, 62.87o, and 69.91o corresponds to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) plane of ZnO, sequentially [25,26]. The ternary composite i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO shows all the characteristics XRD peaks corresponding to N-C-dots and SgCN/ZnO as displayed in Fig. 1.

FTIR assessment was done to determine the kinds of functional groups incorporated in the synthesized NMs as displayed in Fig. 2 . It is evident from Fig. 2 that N-C-dots comprised of various functional groups as revealed by absorption peaks positions at 3408, 2917, 1642, 1590, 1433, 1120, 1016, 878 and 782 cm−1 which corresponds to O-H/N-H, CH, CC, NH/asymmetric COO−, CN/symmetric COO−, asymmetric C-O-C, symmetric C-O-C, various types of CH bonds, and aromatic CH out of the plane deformation/CS stretching, respectively [8,17,27–29]. The in-situ synthesized SgCN/ZnO exhibited characteristic peaks corresponding to both SgCN as well as ZnO. The absorption peaks at 3176, 1652–1208, 1130, 886, 808, and 620 cm−1 are assigned to the presence of OH, heptazine heterocyclic (C6N7) group, CO, deformation of NH, breathing mode of triazine (heterocyclic condensed CN) of S-g-C3N4, and Zn-OH group [26], sequentially [19,30]. The ternary hybrid heterojunction i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO showed various FTIR absorption peaks corresponding to SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots. However, because of major proportion of SgCN in SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO, its absorption peaks are dominant in binary and ternary composites.

Various XPS spectra of synthesized NMs have been displayed in Figs. 3 and 4
. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 (a), N-C-dots are composed of C, N and O elements. N-C-dots' C1s spectra displayed 4 peaks located at 282.36, 285.05, 290.27 and 292.99 eV assigned to CH [31], C=C/CN [32–34], C=O/HO-C=O [35,36] and delocalization of π-electrons [34], sequentially (Fig. 3 (b)), while 2 peaks corresponding to CO [37] and C-OH [32] have been displayed by O1s XPS spectra at 528.37 and 529.49 eV, respectively (Fig. 3 (c)). Moreover, the N1s spectra of C-dots has been resolved into 3 peaks located at 397.06, 398.23 and 399.62 eV which are linked to C-N-C, C-NH2 and NH bonds, sequentially [8,17] (Fig. 3 (d)). The XPS survey spectra of SgCN/ZnO revealed the presence of C, N, O, S and Zn in the synthesized heterojunction as demonstrated in Fig. 4 (a).

The C1s spectrum of SgCN/ZnO exhibited 2 peaks at 285.23 and 288.47 eV associated to carbon impurities and N=C-N, sequentially (Fig. 4 (b)) [8]. Its O1s spectra is also composed of 2 peaks at 529.59 and 532.25 eV corresponding to Zn-O/C-OH and Zn-OH bonds, sequentially (Fig. 4 (c)) [38], while its N1s spectra revealed the existence of C=N-C and N-(C3) (Fig. 4 (d)) [17]. The S2p XPS spectra of SgCN/ZnO has been deconvoluted into 2 peaks at 164.9 and 166.4 eV, assigned to -C-S- and N-S bonds, sequentially [37,39]. The presence of Zn has been detected at 1020.83 and 1044.01 eV ascribed to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, sequentially, in XPS spectra of SgCN/ZnO hybrid nanostructure [10,22,38]. In addition, the presence of all the elements of N-C-dots as well as SgCN/ZnO in survey spectra of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO revealed the successful formation of ternary composite as displayed in Fig. 3 (e) [22].

As displayed in Fig. 5 (a), the resultant N-C-dots demonstrated both the characteristic peaks at 1355 and 1564 cm−1 in Raman spectra. The peak centered at 1355 cm−1, also called D-band is because of the existence of random arrangement of sp3 hybridized carbon-atoms in carbon dots, while a comparatively sharper peak at 1564 cm−1 (G-Band) is ascribed to the presence of schematic arrangement of sp2 hybridized carbon-atoms [40]. The percentage purity of N-C-dots has been determined from the D- and G-bands' intensity ratio and found to be 95.82 % [17].

In addition, SgCN/ZnO exhibited the characteristics peaks related to both of SgCN and ZnO. The less intense peaks at 305, 434 and 502 cm−1 denote hexagonal wurtzite phase of ZnO, and peak at 607 cm−1 is due to oxygen vacancy defect in ZnO as shown in Raman spectra of SgCN/ZnO. Hence these peaks revealed the existence of ZnO in SgCN/ZnO. Additionally, D- and G-bands centered at 1358 and 1572 cm−1 in Raman spectra of SgCN/ZnO correspond to SgCN as represented in Fig. 5 (b) [41,42]. Moreover, N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO exhibited Raman peaks corresponding to both of its individual components i.e., N-C-dots and SgCN/ZnO with little variation in peaks positions.

HRTEM images have been used for morphological characterization of synthesized samples. The HRTEM images of N-C-dots, SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO along with their corresponding size distribution histograms have been displayed in Fig. 6 . The particles of N-C-dots as well as ZnO are quasi-spherical with means diameter of 9.38 and 15.47 nm, sequentially [22]. Additionally, it can be observed from Fig. 6 (c) that SgCN has layered structure. The decoration of N-C-dots over SgCN/ZnO can be seen in N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO [Fig. 6 (c)]. The particle size range and mean size of obtained samples have been tabulated in Table 1 .

The UV-vis absorption spectra in addition to respective Tauc's plots of N-C-dots, SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO has been displayed in Fig. 7 (a) and (b). The obtained N-C-dots exhibited a sharp absorption peak at 307 nm which is ascribed to n-π* movement of electrons (CO or CN) [43,44]. While, SgCN/ZnO demonstrated two peaks, a relatively sharp peak at 318 nm and a broad peak at 385 nm ascribed to π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions of conjugated aromatic s-heptazine cyclic system, sequentially [45]. Furthermore, the decoration of N-C-dots over SgCN/ZnO reduced its band gap from 3.17 eV to 2.89 eV which indicated faster generation of photo-induced electron-hole couples in ternary composite which consequently boost its photocatalytic efficiency.

The characteristics excitation wavelength (λex) dependent PL spectra of N-C-dots has been shown in Fig. 8 (a) with continuous red-shifting of peaks of PL emission plots. It was observed that with increase in λex from 440 to 470 nm the peaks intensity of PL emission plots increased and beyond λex of 470 nm, it started decreasing. Such PL behavior of N-C-dots is likely because of variation in the particles size of C-dots and presence of several emissive regions on C-dots' surface. However, exact mechanism attributed to λex-dependent PL spectra of C-dots is not entirely clear [37]. The PL spectra of SgCN/ZnO and N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO has been displayed in Fig. 8 (b). It is noticed from the Fig. 8 (b) that intensity of PL emission spectra of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO is lower than that of SgCN/ZnO at λex = 360 nm. The lower PL emission spectra intensity of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO indicated the slower recombination rate and greater mobility of charge carriers which further signifies greater photocatalytic dye eradication efficiency of ternary composite [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic potential of produced NMs was examined scientifically by means of photocatalytic decontamination of RhB dye via optimization of several reaction variable including photocatalyst amount, dye concentration and pH of dye solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of distinct fabricated samples on photocatalytic degradation of RhB
Impact of different dosages of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: Another important parameter influencing the photocatalytic breakdown of dyes is photocatalyst dosage. To investigate the influence of photocatalyst quantity on RhB dye elimination, experiments were conducted using distinct (0.03 to 0.09 g) photocatalyst quantities. 0.03–0.09 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO along with 50 mL of RhB dye (10 ppm) were employed to evaluate the effect of photocatalyst quantity on dye decomposition for 75 min as shown in Fig. 10 and tabulated in Table 3 . The degree of dye decomposition improved when the photocatalyst dosage was raised, and it coincidentally attained an optimal value at 0.05 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO. Afterward, the percentage of dye breakdown dropped as shown in Fig. 10(a) and tabulated in Table 3. The extent of active sites on photocatalyst's surface affects how efficient it is at catalyzing reactions. A rise in the dose of the photocatalyst often result in a rise in both the overall surface area as well as degree of surface functional components. As a result, a greater number of dye molecules can be adsorbed on large surface area by extended number of surface functional groups. Both of these conditions led to a greater extent of dye breakdown. Interestingly, a decrease in the percentage of dye removal was observed if the quantity of photocatalyst was raised from 0.05 to 0.09 g. This effect is brought about by reduction in the extent of photoactivated solution volume due to the decrease in UV light absorption brought on by an increase in photocatalyst amount [47,48].

To investigate the influence of dye content, distinct RhB dye solutions are prepared having concentrations of 5, 10, 15, and 20 ppm. 50 mL of every dye solution, 0.05 g of composite, and 75 min of irradiation time were used for the RhB dye decomposition in this investigation. The percentage of dye breakdown was observed to decline as the dye content raised, as tabulated in Table 4 . The optimal decomposition of RhB dye was observed at a concentration of 5 ppm, as illustrated in Fig. 10(b). This is because the photocatalyst's dye-degradation ability is directly correlated with the creation of e−-h+ couples and hence generation of strongly reactive species including O2 • − and •OH on its surface. Furthermore, when dye content in solution increases, the penetration length of the incident photons become short and consequently it leads to lower the photocatalyst's ability to absorb photons leading to a decrease in the photocatalytic efficacy for dye decomposition [17,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of aqueous medium and determination of pHpzc of hybrid nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The point of zero charges (pHpzc) of the photocatalyst can be used to determine the optimum pH for the photocatalytic dye decomposition. When the pH of the solution drops below pHpzc of the photocatalyst, the surface of the photocatalyst gets positively charged and vice-versa [50]. By applying the salt addition approach, the pHpzc of the composite was found to be 2.84 as shown in Fig. 11(a) [17]. This suggests that at pH levels below 2.84, the composite surface turns cationic. Consequently, cationic dye and positively charged photocatalyst are pulled apart by electrostatic repulsive forces which leads to lower photocatalytic dye decomposition. Therefore, the photocatalyst surface needs to be suitably anionic to promote the photocatalytic degradation of cationic RhB dye, as this increases the electrostatic force of attraction between dye molecules and photocatalyst.

The present investigation examined the effect of solution pH on dye eradication utilizing 0.05 g of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO and 50 mL of 5 ppm of different pH (1.70 to 9.5) of RhB dye solution. It has been noticed that the degree of dye decomposition enhanced initially with increasing solution pH, reached its maximum, and subsequently dropped, as shown in Fig. 11(b) and summarized in Table 5 . This maximum is attained at pH = 3.5 [51]. It should be highlighted that N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO showed the greatest decomposition of RhB dye in only 35 min at pH of 3.5 aqueous medium. This indicates that at pH = 3.5, the rate of RhB breakdown become highest and the time duration required for the photocatalytic RhB dye decomposition dropped. However, the efficacy of the photocatalyst is decreased if the pH of RhB dye solutions is higher than 3.5. The development of a thin layer on photocatalyst surface likely caused by the increased extent of adsorption of cationic RhB dye molecules on the strongly negatively charged photocatalyst's surface at strongly alkaline medium reduces photocatalyst effectiveness [52]. At higher pH values, this layer acts as a barrier to stop irradiated UV rays from approaching the catalyst surface, resulting in reduced decomposition of the dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of reaction kinetics of photocatalytic RhB dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Employing the most efficient synthesized NMs, viz. N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO, the photocatalytic removal of RhB dyes is demonstrated in Fig. 12 .

A reduction in dye content is denoted by absorption peak's intensity decreasing with irradiation time. Additionally, RhB dye demonstrated negligible breakdown when exposed to light alone as well as under dark conditions as displayed in Fig. 12 (b). The linearly fitted plot of ln(Ao/At) with irradiation time, as shown in Fig. 13 , showed the first-order kinetics for photocatalytic dye decomposition. The correlation coefficient (R2) data also provide evidence for first-order kinetics [53]. For the first-order kinetics, the values of R2 for the linear fitted plot varies from 0.95 to 1 (Table 6 ). The first-order reaction and its half-life are studied using the following formulae [8,17]:

The ‘k’ value for the RhB breakdown have been calculated to be 0.08035 min−1. Using N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO photocatalysts, the percentage of RhB dye removal is computed using the following equation:

Moreover, the following formulas are used to compute the electricity cost (EC) for detoxifying RhB dye [Table 7 ] [54]:

At our work place, the cost of electricity is 4.68 (INR)/unit, having a permitted consumption limit of 500 units. Improved dye eradication efficiency lowers the overall electrical expenses for the dye degradation and increases the photocatalyst financial sustainability. As mentioned earlier, the photocatalytic effectiveness of the synthesized samples is N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO > SgCN/ZnO > N-C-dots. Thus, the most economical and efficient photocatalyst is N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO hybrid nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability studies of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO hybrid nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Studies were carried out for both purposes i.e., ensuring the long-term sustainability and feasibility of the most efficient photocatalyst i.e., N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO. The resulting sample showed a remarkable reusable characteristic up to 5 cycles as shown in Fig. 14(a) with just a small reduction in its effectiveness for dye elimination; which is probably due to the photocatalyst's continuous weight reduction after each successive cycle. To evaluate the stability of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO, XRD examination was performed prior to and subsequent to 5 photocatalytic rounds and the results obtained are demonstrated in Fig. 14(b) Only slight variations have been observed in XRD pattern of the freshly produced and recycled sample, indicating exceptional composite stability [55,56]. It is important to compare the advantages/performance of the synthesized NMs with the existing literature [57,58]. Therefore, the comparison of photocatalytic dye degradation efficiency of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO with similar studies has been provided in Table 8 which makes it evident that N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO used in this investigation exhibited higher photocatalytic RhB dye degradation effectiveness than those reported in other similar studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of different scavenger on RhB photocatalytic decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: In aqueous solution, a photocatalyst subjected to light irradiations usually generates 3 kinds of predominant reactive radicals: h+, •O2 − and •OH radicals [69]. These photoreactive components efficiently aid in dye remediation via photocatalysis. Investigations on the entrapment of reactive radicals were carried out to gain a thorough grasp of the function of these primary reactive radicals in the photocatalytic remediation of dye-contaminated wastewater. Using 5 mL of 10 mM of p-BZQ, tertiary-butyl alcohol (t-BA) and Na2EDTA for O2 • −, •OH and h+ scavenging, respectively, the distinct trapping experiments were carried out. It has been observed that RhB photocatalytic decomposition was significantly suppressed by p-BZQ via the entrapment of O2 • −, whereas the addition of t-BA and Na2EDTA to the dye solution for the entrapment of h+ and •OH decreased the RhB photocatalytic deterioration to lesser extent (Fig. 16 ) [70]. When p-BZQ, t-BA, and Na2EDTA were added to dye solution, the percentage of photocatalytic dye elimination dropped to 12.45 %, 77.58 %, and 85.69 % for RhB dye, respectively. This indicates that O2 • − renders the primary participation in photocatalytic RhB decomposition, whereas •OH and h+ have minor involvement. Thus, the sequence in which these reactive components participated in RhB photocatalytic decomposition is O2 • − > •OH > h+.

Sweet potato derived N-C-dots by pyrolysis approach and in-situ produced SgCN/ZnO are hydrothermally treated to develop their ternary hybrid nanocomposite N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO. In contrast to the in-situ produced SgCN/ZnO, which has a porous layering framework with ZnO nanoparticles adorned on it, the doped-C-dots were nearly spherical and crystalline in nature. The resulting NMs were investigated for photocatalytic RhB decontamination along with determination of rate constant, % RhB decomposition, half-life, electrical energy per order, and costs of electricity. Furthermore, several variables including photocatalyst dosage, concentration and pH of dye solution have been optimized. The developed ternary composite N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated 93.49 % photocatalytic RhB decomposition under optimized circumstances (0.05 g photocatalyst dosage, 5 ppm dye content, and pH = 3.5). Enhancement of N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO photocatalytic efficacy is because of the reduction in reintegration of e−-h+ couples following the incorporation of doped-C-dots with SgCN/ZnO. Moreover, PL examination revealed that the N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO displayed reduced electron-hole reunion rate and significantly faster-charged particle's movement, which promotes a greater degree of dye breakdown. The results of the entrapment investigations showed that O2 • − radicals serve as the predominant species, whilst h+ and •OH radicals had smaller participation in the photocatalytic breakdown of RhB. Furthermore, N-C-dots/SgCN/ZnO demonstrated good stability and reusability up to five rounds with only a slight decrement in its dye decontamination capability. This study revealed that incorporating N-C-dots over SgCN/ZnO is an efficient method for effective decontamination of RhB dye.
